title: Neurofibromatosis Type 2 -- Evaluation
content: Multiple spinal tumors can also be seen in patients with NF2, including schwannomas, meningiomas, and ependymomas, and can be diagnosed with MRI. Schwannomas are the most common type; they typically arise from the dorsal root with the typical appearance of a dumbbell shape with the widening of the neural foramen and intense enhancement. Meningiomas enhance extramedullary lesions, typically seen in the cervical or thoracic region. Ependymomas are intramedullary lesions causing enlargement of the spinal cord with hemorrhage, cystic changes, and variable enhancement. [7]

Referral to specialists such as neurologists and surgeons as appropriate will also be critical for the appropriate monitoring of tumors and guiding the patient towards an optimal outcome.

title: Vestibular Schwannoma -- History and Physical
content: Schwannomas in the extremities may present as an asymptomatic mass, mild pain in the area, or paresthesia due to pressure on the parent nerve. Sciatic nerve schwannomas can mimic disk herniation with low back pain with radiation down the leg. Schwannoma, which involves the C7 nerve root, can produce thoracic outlet syndrome. A schwannoma in the ankle or wrist can present as tarsal tunnel syndrome or carpal tunnel syndrome. The presentation of distal symptoms can be due to a lesion in a proximal nerve. Schwannoma may trigger a physiological deficit due to local pressure on the originating nerve.

title: Neurofibromatosis -- Continuing Education Activity
content: NF1 is characterized by the presence of neurofibromas, cafe-au-lait spots, freckling, optic gliomas, and bony dysplasias. Bilateral vestibular schwannomas and meningiomas are characteristic of NF2; however, neurofibromas do not occur in this syndrome. Therefore, the name NF2 is a misnomer, and a new and more accurate name for NF2-related schwannomatosis has been established based on an international consensus recommendation. Neurofibromatosis types 1 and 2 treatment involves clinical monitoring and medical intervention when appropriate. Schwannomatosis is the rarest form, marked by multiple peripheral and spinal schwannomas, often causing chronic pain. Vestibular involvement is uncommon, differentiating it from NF2.

title: Neurofibromatosis Type 1 -- Treatment / Management
content: Tumors are treated based on the type, involvement of other structures, and associated complications. Radiation therapy in NF-1 is associated with the emergence of secondary tumors, thereby opting for radiation only in essential cases. [53] Cutaneous and nodular neurofibromas are treated when they develop symptoms like pain, increase in size, weakness, vision loss, or cosmetic concerns. Surgery, laser, and electrodesiccation are some of the treatment modalities. Gabapentin has sometimes been utilized for pruritus treatment along with neuropathic pain management. In particular, plexiform neurofibromas (PN) are associated with high morbidity and malignant transformation compared to cutaneous and nodular neurofibromas. Compression of the airway or spinal cord and disfigurement are common reasons for the surgical removal of plexiform neurofibromas. [54] Selumetinib was approved for use in children three years of age and older with PN that cannot be operated on despite having symptoms or being noted to be making progress. Selumetinib is the first drug approved by US food and drug administration for plexiform NF. [43] It is a mitogen-activated protein kinase inhibitor. [55]

title: Neurofibromatosis Type 2 -- Treatment / Management
content: Small asymptomatic vestibular schwannomas can be managed conservatively with MRI follow-up. Surgery is the primary treatment for large symptomatic vestibular schwannomas. Meningiomas are also treated surgically, with radiation treatment reserved for non-surgical candidates.[8] Spinal cord ependymomas are usually low-grade tumors and can be followed clinically, with surgery reserved for symptomatic patients.

title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Meninges -- Surgical Considerations
content: There are many tumors of the spinal meninges, one example being a meningioma. While the majority of meningiomas appear in the cranial meninges, they are also known to grow in the spinal portions, especially in the thoracic and cervical portions. Spinal meningiomas are typically benign and slow-growing and typically occur in women aged 50 to 70. Children with neurofibromatosis type 2 are also susceptible to developing spinal cord tumors, such as meningiomas and intramedullary tumors. [15] [16]

title: Conus and Cauda Equina Tumors -- Introduction
content: Primary spinal cord tumors are less common than metastatic spinal tumors. Tumors of the spinal canal are classified in 3 ways depending on the tissue compartment in which they are found. Extra-dural: arising outside of the dura mater Intra-dural/Extra-medullary: arising within the dura mater but outside of the neural tissue Intra-dural/Intra-medullary: arising within the dura mater and within the substance of the neural tissue

title: Conus and Cauda Equina Tumors -- Treatment / Management
content: Surgical indications are aimed at decompression of the neural elements, gross tumor resection if possible, and stabilization of the spine, should there be any instability. As always, a multidisciplinary approach is best for determining the appropriate treatment based on long-term prognosis, as well as other indicated systemic treatments. Schwannoma and meningioma lesions are usually benign. [10] [11] [12] If schwannoma or meningioma is suspected and the patient is asymptomatic, observation is usually appropriate if the lesion is small. However, should there be a large lesion or neurological symptoms, then surgical resection for decompression and gross total resection should be attempted.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1713
content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology within various anatomical regions. Specific operations that are classified as methods of exploration include partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, whether accompanied by cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, is regarded as an exploration method. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Orthopedic exploration of the spinal canal is achieved through laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; this technique is applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and sacral regions, and may be listed as a possibly included method in broader procedural codes. Additional exploration procedures encompass nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, which can be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, including specific transthoracic mediastinal exploration. Collectively, these diverse surgical methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ system and clinical objective, serving both diagnostic and therapeutic roles in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly inspect, access, or remove pathology from specific anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland surgery such as adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by several cholecystectomy variants that incorporate common duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, each designed to evaluate and treat ductal stones or strictures. Cranial exploration is covered by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, which allow direct visualization of intracranial lesions. Orbital pathology can be addressed through a transcranial approach to explore the orbit and excise lesions. Spinal exploration procedures include laminectomies—cervical, sacral, lumbar, thoracic, or multilevel—performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina in conditions such as spinal stenosis; these may be listed as core methods or as possibly included techniques depending on the extent of vertebral involvement. A related laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) technique provides nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated disc material at a single lumbar level, often as a re‑exploration. Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach exemplifies neck and mediastinal access for hyperparathyroidism. All of these specific methods fall under the broader “Exploration procedure” classification, which itself is organized under the parent category “Clinical actions A‑G.” This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse surgical explorations are grouped for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

Spinal exploration includes cervical, thoracic, lumbar, and sacral laminectomy/laminotomy techniques that provide exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; single‑segment procedures involve one to two vertebral levels, whereas multi‑segment procedures involve more than two levels. Excision is the operative method underlying many of these exploration procedures and serves as the parent concept for “Clinical actions A‑G,” which in turn parents the broader Exploration procedure class. title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly examine an anatomic region to diagnose, treat, or remove pathology. The category includes adrenalectomy—partial or complete removal of the adrenal gland performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy—and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, is classified as an exploration method, as is choledochotomy or choledochostomy performed for ductal exploration, drainage, or stone removal, sometimes accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by craniectomy or craniotomy performed for supratentorial investigation and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Spinal exploration procedures consist of laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; these are specified for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and thoracic regions, and for more than two thoracic segments, with the latter variations noted as possibly included methods. Urologic and endocrine exploration includes radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration. The broader classification “Clinical actions A‑G” serves as a parent category for exploration procedures, indicating that these diverse surgical methods are organized under a common clinical action framework. Together, these procedures illustrate how direct anatomical exploration is integral to the diagnosis, management, and definitive treatment of diseases across multiple organ systems.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 620
content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to diagnose, assess, and treat pathology across multiple anatomical regions. In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—performed partially or completely via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, with or without biopsy and occasionally combined with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors—serves both therapeutic removal and exploratory evaluation of adrenal disease. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common duct exploration, which may be performed laparoscopically or with intra‑operative cholangiography, and choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate ductal drainage, calculus removal, and adjunctive transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by suboccipital craniectomy for cranial nerve decompression and transcranial orbital exploration for lesion excision. Spinal exploration encompasses a spectrum of laminectomy techniques—cervical, lumbar, sacral, and thoracic—designed to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy, as well as more extensive multilevel thoracic laminectomies and laminotomies (hemilaminectomies) that include nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, and disc excision. Additional specialized explorations include nipple exploration with lactiferous duct excision, parathyroidectomy with or without mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration. Collectively, these methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ and clinical objective, often integrating diagnostic biopsy, lesion removal, drainage, or decompression to optimize patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide range of operative techniques across several organ systems. It encompasses adrenalectomy procedures—both partial and complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, including cases with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, which may be combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Excision also covers exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, and various laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) techniques for cervical and lumbar nerve‑root decompression that may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is another method classified under Excision, along with multiple forms of parathyroidectomy—standard cervical parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches, and parathyroidectomy combined with mediastinal exploration. Excision is further linked to “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” and is associated with plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its inclusion in the broader grouping “Clinical actions A‑G.” However, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, notably laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy (e.g., procedures for spinal stenosis involving one or two vertebral segments). This integrated overview delineates the diverse methods that fall under Excision, clarifies which procedures are excluded, and highlights its relevance for procedural classification and patient‑care planning.
